title: Could the high-mass black holes from gravitational-wave observations be explained by lensing?
comment: 11 pages, 11 figures
summary: The high-mass ($M \gtrsim 30 M_\odot$) black holes (BHs) from the gravitational-wave (GW) observations of LIGO and Virgo came as a surprise to many astronomers. While the collapse of metal-poor massive stars could produce such BHs, gravitational lensing has been invoked to explain their high masses. Broadhurst, Diego, and Smoot (henceforth BDS) argued that the mass distribution of BHs in coalescing binaries is very similar to that of the galactic BHs, and the inferred high masses are the result of neglecting the lensing magnification. They also proposed a redshift distribution of binary BH (BBH) mergers to explain the observed LIGO-Virgo mass distribution. We ask whether such a model is consistent with different aspects of the GW observations: 1) the observed number of BBH mergers, 2) the distribution of their redshifted total mass and apparent luminosity distance, 3) the non-detection of strongly lensed events, and 4) the non-observation of the stochastic GW background. By simulating lensed BBH mergers with the BDS model and comparing them with observations, we conclude that no choice of BDS model parameters is consistent with all aspects of the observations. Lensing magnification is not a viable explanation for the high-mass BHs discovered by LIGO and Virgo.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether gravitational lensing can explain high-mass black holes in LIGO/Virgo observations and finds the lensing magnification model inconsistent with multiple observational constraints.
AI.motivation: To test the hypothesis by Broadhurst, Diego, and Smoot (BDS) that gravitational lensing magnification explains the high masses of black holes observed by LIGO and Virgo, rather than requiring metal-poor massive star collapse.
AI.method: Simulate lensed binary black hole (BBH) mergers using the BDS model and compare with four observational constraints: 1) number of observed BBH mergers, 2) distribution of redshifted total mass and apparent luminosity distance, 3) non-detection of strongly lensed events, and 4) non-observation of stochastic gravitational wave background.
AI.result: No choice of BDS model parameters is consistent with all observational aspects. The lensing magnification hypothesis fails to simultaneously satisfy all four constraints from GW observations.
AI.conclusion: Gravitational lensing is not a viable explanation for the high-mass black holes discovered by LIGO and Virgo. The high masses likely require alternative explanations such as metal-poor massive star collapse.

title: Deep Imaging Meets Motion: Complementing Stream Photometry Through Planetary Nebula Kinematics
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, submitted to the proceedings of IAU Symposium 403
summary: The combination of deep imaging data and kinematic measurements in galaxy outskirts promises to reveal extensive insights into the structure and history of individual galaxies. From a census of tidal features around galaxies from the Magneticum simulation, we disentangle the dynamics for a selected stellar stream from the underlying halo by identifying the stream progenitor galaxy. While these dynamics are challenging to measure observationally, we show that they are effectively obtained through planetary nebulae (PNe) as tracers, which we model in the simulation using the PN framework PICS (PNe In Cosmological Simulations). We find that the PNe in the brightest 1.5 mag of their luminosity function are sufficient to recover the underlying stellar dynamics of the massive stream. We thereby establish PNe as an attractive alternative to expensive deep IFU observations, where combining low-surface-brightness observations and PN dynamical measurements will enhance our ability to constrain the gravitational potential of galaxies.

AI.tldr: Using Magneticum cosmological simulations, researchers demonstrate that planetary nebulae (PNe) can effectively trace stellar dynamics in galaxy outskirts, providing an alternative to expensive deep IFU observations for studying tidal streams and gravitational potentials.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address the challenge of measuring dynamics in galaxy outskirts observationally, where tidal features contain valuable information about galaxy structure and history but are difficult to study with traditional methods.
AI.method: The researchers use the Magneticum cosmological simulation to study tidal features around galaxies, identify stream progenitor galaxies, and model planetary nebulae (PNe) using the PICS (PNe In Cosmological Simulations) framework to trace stellar dynamics.
AI.result: The study finds that PNe in the brightest 1.5 magnitudes of their luminosity function are sufficient to recover the underlying stellar dynamics of massive streams, establishing PNe as effective tracers for studying galaxy outskirts.
AI.conclusion: Planetary nebulae provide an attractive alternative to expensive deep integral field unit (IFU) observations, and combining low-surface-brightness observations with PN dynamical measurements will enhance our ability to constrain the gravitational potential of galaxies.

title: Cold vs. Hot Gas Accretion and Angular Momentum in FIRE Simulations: From Halo to Galaxy Scales
comment: 25 pages, 19 figures. Submitted to MNRAS
summary: We present a systematic study of gas accretion and angular momentum in the circumgalactic medium (CGM) using high-resolution FIRE cosmological simulations. Our analysis includes halos spanning the critical $\sim 10^{12}\ \mathrm{M}_{\odot}$ scale where several transitions have been identified, including inner CGM virialization, the transition from bursty to steady star formation, and the emergence of thin disks. We find that the temperature of inflowing gas is correlated with the virialization of the inner CGM. CGM inflows are almost entirely cold ($T < 10^5$ K) in pre-virialized halos, while hot inflows ($T > 10^5$ K) dominate in virialized halos. When hot inflows dominate, cooling generally occurs simultaneously with circularization at galaxy radii. The dominance of hot inflows onto massive galaxies persists even at high redshift, where cold streams may coexist. Consistent with previous studies, cold inflows have higher specific angular momentum than dark matter and hot gas. However, in bursty, low-mass galaxies, cold inflows do not circularize prior to star formation, while in steady, massive galaxies, hot inflows circularize, cool, and form stars with disk-like kinematics. We additionally find that in bursty galaxies, accreted gas typically forms stars after residing in the galaxy for less than $\sim 5$ galaxy free-fall times, while in steady galaxies, gas can persist for up to $\sim 25$ free-fall times before forming stars. This highlights a key difference between star formation in bursty galaxies fed by cold accretion and steady equilibrium disks fed by hot accretion.

AI.tldr: FIRE simulations reveal that gas accretion in galaxies depends on halo mass: cold inflows dominate in low-mass, bursty galaxies while hot inflows dominate in massive, steady galaxies with virialized CGM, affecting star formation timescales and disk formation.
AI.motivation: To systematically study gas accretion and angular momentum in the circumgalactic medium across the critical halo mass scale (~10^12 M_sun) where multiple galaxy transitions occur, including CGM virialization, star formation mode changes, and disk emergence.
AI.method: High-resolution FIRE cosmological simulations analyzing halos spanning the critical mass scale, examining temperature distributions of inflowing gas, angular momentum properties, and star formation timescales in different galaxy regimes.
AI.result: 1) Inflow temperature correlates with inner CGM virialization: cold inflows (T < 10^5 K) dominate pre-virialized halos, hot inflows (T > 10^5 K) dominate virialized halos. 2) Cold inflows have higher specific angular momentum than dark matter and hot gas. 3) In bursty galaxies, cold inflows don't circularize before star formation; in steady galaxies, hot inflows circularize, cool, and form stars with disk kinematics. 4) Star formation timescales differ: bursty galaxies ~5 free-fall times, steady galaxies up to ~25 free-fall times.
AI.conclusion: Two distinct accretion modes exist: bursty galaxies fed by cold accretion with rapid star formation, and steady equilibrium disks fed by hot accretion with delayed star formation and disk-like kinematics, highlighting fundamental differences in galaxy formation pathways across the critical mass scale.

title: Stream on: Evolution of stellar shells and streams - A case study
comment: 8 pages, 3 figures, submitted to Proceedings IAU Symposium No. 403
summary: Tidal stellar shells and streams are two of the most intriguing low-surface-brightness features within galaxies, consisting of stars accreted from satellite galaxies. A crucial ingredient in determining which type of feature will be formed is the orbit of the satellite galaxy. Additionally, the distribution of stars from these satellite galaxies within the merger remnant and the original location of these stars within the progenitor satellite galaxy provide important clues about the deposition of the stellar component in the resulting galaxy. We utilize the cosmological hydrodynamical simulation Magneticum Pathfinder and expand on the work by Valenzuela & Remus (2024) and Stoiber et al. (2025) to present a case study for the formation of a stream and a shell system. We analyze their orbits and the distributions of stellar particles within their host galaxy and compare them to their initial location within the progenitor satellite galaxy. We find that the orbit of the stream progenitor is more circular than the progenitor of the shell system. The stellar particles of the stream from different initial radii are found at roughly the same distances with respect to the host galaxy. However, the part of the stream visible in mock observations - not hidden by the host galaxy - consists of stars from within the core of the progenitor ($r/r_{1/2} < 1$). On the other hand, the stellar particles of the shell system retain their radial ordering: Stars that were initially at small radii in the satellite galaxy also remain closer to the center of the host galaxy.

AI.tldr: Analysis of tidal stellar shells and streams formation using cosmological simulations reveals orbital differences and stellar distribution patterns: streams form from more circular orbits with mixed stellar origins, while shells maintain radial ordering from progenitor satellite.
AI.motivation: To understand the formation mechanisms of tidal stellar shells and streams in galaxies, particularly how satellite galaxy orbits and initial stellar distributions within progenitors determine which type of feature forms and how stars are deposited in merger remnants.
AI.method: Utilized the cosmological hydrodynamical simulation Magneticum Pathfinder, building on previous work by Valenzuela & Remus (2024) and Stoiber et al. (2025). Conducted a case study analyzing orbits of stream and shell progenitors, distributions of stellar particles within host galaxies, and comparison to initial locations within progenitor satellite galaxies.
AI.result: Stream progenitor orbits are more circular than shell system progenitors. Stream stellar particles from different initial radii mix to similar distances in host galaxy, but visible stream portions consist of stars from progenitor cores (r/r₁/₂ < 1). Shell system stellar particles maintain radial ordering: stars initially at small radii in satellite remain closer to host galaxy center.
AI.conclusion: Orbital characteristics of satellite galaxies crucially determine whether streams or shells form, with streams resulting from more circular orbits and shells from different orbital configurations. The preservation or mixing of radial ordering from progenitor satellites provides important clues about stellar deposition mechanisms in galaxy mergers.

title: The Damping and Instability of Ion-acoustic Waves in the Solar Wind: Solar Orbiter Observations
comment: Accepted for publication by ApJ. Code used in this work is available under the MIT license here: https://github.com/RanHao1999/SWA-Data-Analysis
summary: Observations of solar wind velocity distribution functions (VDFs) commonly reveal fine-scale structures. These features strongly influence kinetic processes such as wave damping and instability, yet their role remains poorly understood. We use a Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to separate proton and $α$-particle (fully ionized helium) VDFs from Solar Orbiter Proton and Alpha-particle Sensor (PAS) measurements, and assess how measured VDFs affect the damping of compressive fluctuations with the Arbitrary Linear Plasma Solver (ALPS). We analyze the dispersion relation and polarization properties of ion-acoustic (IA) waves in the solar wind. Protons and $α$-particles are represented by the measured VDFs derived from PAS observations. For comparison, we also perform calculations using the bi-Maxwellian assumption for the VDFs. Fine-scale structures of the measured proton VDFs reduce the damping rate of IA waves, even when $T_e \simeq T_i$. In some cases, we find that the measured VDFs drive the IA mode unstable, while the corresponding bi-Maxwellian representations predict strong damping. These results demonstrate that resolving the fine-scale structures of VDFs is essential for accurately capturing the kinetic physics of the solar wind.

AI.tldr: Gaussian Mixture Model analysis of Solar Orbiter PAS data shows that fine-scale structures in proton VDFs reduce ion-acoustic wave damping and can drive instability, unlike bi-Maxwellian approximations.
AI.motivation: Fine-scale structures in solar wind velocity distribution functions (VDFs) strongly influence kinetic processes like wave damping and instability, but their role remains poorly understood and requires investigation beyond bi-Maxwellian approximations.
AI.method: Used Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM) to separate proton and α-particle VDFs from Solar Orbiter Proton and Alpha-particle Sensor (PAS) measurements, then analyzed ion-acoustic wave damping using Arbitrary Linear Plasma Solver (ALPS) with both measured VDFs and bi-Maxwellian representations for comparison.
AI.result: Fine-scale structures in measured proton VDFs reduce damping rate of ion-acoustic waves even when Te ≈ Ti. In some cases, measured VDFs drive IA mode unstable while corresponding bi-Maxwellian representations predict strong damping.
AI.conclusion: Resolving fine-scale structures of VDFs is essential for accurately capturing kinetic physics of solar wind, as bi-Maxwellian approximations can misrepresent wave damping and stability properties.

title: Upper bound of ejecta mass in a nova outburst
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures, 1 table, accepted for publication in ApJ
summary: We present the maximum ejecta mass $(M_{\rm ej})_{\rm max}$ and the maximum ratio of ejecta mass and accreted mass $(M_{\rm ej}/M_{\rm acc})_{\rm max}$ of a nova for various white dwarf (WD) masses ($M_{\rm WD}=0.6$ - 1.38 $M_\odot$) and mass accretion rates ($\dot{M}_{\rm acc}=1\times 10^{-11}$ - $3\times 10^{-7} ~M_\odot$ yr$^{-1}$) based on the energy balance with nuclear burning. These maximum values serve as an upper bound of mass ejection for individual novae. Recently, B. E. Schaefer concluded that the WD masses in the recurrent novae U Sco and T CrB decreased at nova explosions, because the ejected mass is much larger than the accreted mass, i.e., $M_{\rm ej}/M_{\rm acc}= 26$ and $540$, respectively. These values are derived from the orbital period change at the nova explosions. Recurrent novae have been considered to be a progenitor system of Type Ia supernovae (SNe Ia) because their WD masses are now close to, and will possibly grow up to, 1.38 $M_\odot$ at which WDs explode as SNe Ia. From the different view point of energy generation at the thermonuclear runaway, we have obtained the much smaller value of the maximum ratio of $M_{\rm ej}/M_{\rm acc}\lesssim 2.6$ for a $1.37 ~M_\odot$ WD. This conclusion simply means that the nuclear (hydrogen) burning cannot release energy enough to expel such a large ejecta mass as B. E. Schaefer's claims. We also conclude that $(M_{\rm ej}/M_{\rm acc})_{\rm max}$ hardly increases even if we include the effect of frictional mass ejection process in the common envelope phase of a nova.

AI.tldr: The paper presents maximum ejecta mass and ejecta-to-accreted mass ratios for novae across WD masses and accretion rates, challenging previous claims of extremely high mass ejection ratios in recurrent novae.
AI.motivation: To establish theoretical upper bounds on nova mass ejection based on energy balance with nuclear burning, and to challenge previous observational claims of extremely high ejecta-to-accreted mass ratios (26 and 540) in recurrent novae U Sco and T CrB, which have implications for WD mass evolution and Type Ia supernova progenitor scenarios.
AI.method: Based on energy balance with nuclear burning, the authors calculate maximum ejecta mass (M_ej_max) and maximum ratio of ejecta mass to accreted mass (M_ej/M_acc_max) for various white dwarf masses (0.6-1.38 M_sun) and mass accretion rates (1×10^-11 to 3×10^-7 M_sun/yr). The analysis includes consideration of frictional mass ejection in common envelope phases.
AI.result: The maximum ratio M_ej/M_acc is much smaller than previously claimed values - less than ~2.6 for a 1.37 M_sun WD, compared to Schaefer's claims of 26 and 540 for U Sco and T CrB. The nuclear burning cannot release enough energy to expel such large ejecta masses. Including frictional mass ejection in common envelope phases does not significantly increase these maximum ratios.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical energy balance constraints show that nuclear burning cannot produce the extremely high ejecta-to-accreted mass ratios claimed from orbital period changes in recurrent novae. This challenges the interpretation that WD masses are decreasing in these systems and has implications for understanding recurrent novae as Type Ia supernova progenitors.

title: The Last Galactic Firework: Timing the last significant merger with stars, globular clusters and $ω$Centauri
comment: Submitted to MNRAS, 11 pages, 7 figures
summary: We present a robust method to empirically infer the timing of the last significant merger in the Milky Way which is tested against fully cosmological models of galaxy formation. We apply it to Milky Way subgiant stars with spectro-photometric ages, finding that the last significant merger (Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus, GSE), occurred $\sim11\,$Gyrs ago. This coincides with the birth of a coeval in-situ group of globular clusters (GCs), which constrains the merger-induced starburst (hereafter {\it Tainá}) to have occurred at $11.2\pm 0.1\,\rm{Gyr}$, the most precise dating of this merger event. The GSE's most metal-rich GCs were also born around this time ($τ=10.9\pm0.1\,\rm{Gyr}$) and likely formed during the merger interaction prior to disruption of the GSE. We argue that $ω$ Centauri is the most likely candidate for the surviving remnant of the GSE, and show that its stellar populations have final ages and metallicities consistent with the GSE GCs together with observational evidence it may have been affected by bar resonances. Furthermore, we argue that the mean metallicity for which stellar orbits transition from halo-like to disc-like kinematics shows an upward inflexion point at $[\rm{Fe/H}]\sim-1.33$, and this sets an upper-limit for the age when the disc was forming. To corroborate this, we identify proto-MW GCs with highly disc-like orbits that formed before the last significant merger (with ages up to $τ=13.0\pm0.5\,\rm{Gyr}$). This places the disc formation time as far back as as $z_{\rm disc\, form}\gtrsim4$.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a method to date the Milky Way's last major merger (Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus) at ~11 Gyr ago using subgiant stars, linking it to coeval globular cluster formation and identifying ω Centauri as the likely surviving remnant.
AI.motivation: To precisely date the last significant merger in the Milky Way's history (Gaia-Sausage-Enceladus) and understand its impact on the formation of globular clusters and the early disc.
AI.method: Empirical inference method tested against cosmological galaxy formation models, applied to Milky Way subgiant stars with spectro-photometric ages. Analysis of globular cluster ages, metallicities, and kinematics to constrain merger timing and disc formation.
AI.result: GSE merger occurred ~11 Gyr ago (11.2±0.1 Gyr), coinciding with birth of coeval in-situ globular clusters. ω Centauri identified as likely surviving GSE remnant. Disc formation began as early as z≳4 (up to 13.0±0.5 Gyr ago).
AI.conclusion: The Milky Way's last major merger (GSE) occurred precisely 11.2±0.1 Gyr ago, triggering a starburst that formed coeval globular clusters. ω Centauri is the probable surviving remnant, and disc formation began much earlier than previously thought, at z≳4.

title: Revisiting Ca II Activity Indices in FGK Stars: Systematic Biases in Infrared Triplet Measurements
comment: 22 pages, 18 figures, submitted to ApJS
summary: Synthetic-template subtraction is widely used to measure chromospheric activity in large spectroscopic surveys. However, many solar-like FGK stars show systematically negative Ca II infrared triplet (IRT) residual indices, implying that the observed line cores are deeper than those predicted by parameter-matched templates. We investigate this effect using solar-like stars from LAMOST DR9, MaStar, and XSL DR3, measuring activity indices (R+) for both the Ca II H&K and IRT lines in a uniform framework. We find that observational effects, including atmospheric-parameter offsets, treatment of the instrumental line-spread function, and propagated measurement uncertainties, contribute to scatter but do not explain the systematic negative bias in R+_IRT. The results instead suggest that the negative bias most likely arises because photospheric templates underestimate the depth of the IRT cores, likely owing to missing chromospheric structure and, to a lesser extent, NLTE effects. An empirical increase in the adopted microturbulent velocity deepens the synthetic IRT cores and partially mitigates the negative offset. In addition, R+ values derived from different synthesis configurations show systematic offsets but generally preserve strong linear correlations, indicating that they can be cross-calibrated. These results clarify the origin of negative Ca II IRT residual indices and help interpret template-dependent systematics in chromospheric activity measurements based on synthetic-template subtraction.

AI.tldr: Systematic negative bias in Ca II IRT residual indices arises from photospheric templates underestimating line core depths due to missing chromospheric structure and NLTE effects, not observational artifacts.
AI.motivation: To investigate why many solar-like FGK stars show systematically negative Ca II infrared triplet residual indices in synthetic-template subtraction methods, which contradicts expectations that observed line cores should be shallower than templates due to chromospheric emission.
AI.method: Analyzed solar-like stars from LAMOST DR9, MaStar, and XSL DR3 using uniform framework to measure activity indices (R+) for both Ca II H&K and IRT lines. Examined observational effects (atmospheric-parameter offsets, instrumental line-spread function treatment, measurement uncertainties) and tested empirical adjustments to microturbulent velocity.
AI.result: Observational effects contribute to scatter but don't explain systematic negative bias. Negative bias likely arises because photospheric templates underestimate IRT core depths due to missing chromospheric structure and NLTE effects. Increasing microturbulent velocity deepens synthetic IRT cores and partially mitigates the offset. R+ values from different synthesis configurations show systematic offsets but preserve strong linear correlations.
AI.conclusion: The systematic negative Ca II IRT residual indices originate from template deficiencies rather than observational artifacts, clarifying template-dependent systematics in chromospheric activity measurements and enabling cross-calibration of different synthesis configurations.

title: The GECKOS survey: Resolving the molecular and ionised gas in the galactic outflow of ESO~484-036
comment: 20 pages, 11 main + 2 appendix figures, Submitted to MNRAS
summary: We present a spatially resolved, multiphase study of the outflow in the edge-on starburst galaxy ESO~484-036 from the GECKOS survey, combining VLT/MUSE H$α$ and ALMA CO(1$-$0) observations to analyse the atomic ionised and cold molecular gas. Both show extraplanar emission consistent with a conical outflow. Ionised gas is enclosed by molecular gas, which is detected up to 2.5 kpc from the disc. Molecular gas dominates near the disc, except at the nuclear base, while ionised gas extends beyond 3 kpc. The deprojected outflow velocities are $\lesssim400\ \rm km\ s^{-1}$ in both phases and are consistent with ballistic motion, with some gas possibly falling back onto the disc. We find that the mass outflow rates are in the range of $\dot M_{\rm ion}\sim1-5\ \rm M_\odot\ \rm yr^{-1}$ and $\dot M_{\rm mol}\sim13-54\ \rm M_\odot\ \rm yr^{-1}$, giving mass loading factors of $η_{M\rm, ion}\sim 0.1-0.6$ and $η_{M\rm, mol}\sim 1.5-6.2$. These ranges reflect velocity and geometric uncertainties. Despite the short depletion time ($τ_{\rm dep} = 16-48\rm\ Myr$), the outflow may regulate rather than permanently quench the gas reservoir. Energy loading ($η_E\leq0.16$) and momentum loading ($η_p\lesssim1$) support a purely starburst-driven outflow. Comparing ESO~484-036 with a literature sample, we find a systematic 1~dex shift in mass-loading relations when molecular gas is included. This produces a $\sim3.5$~dex discrepancy with cosmological simulations in $η_{M\rm, mol}/η_{M\rm, ion}$, implying that current models strongly underpredict cold gas production and the role of short-range recycling flows in starburst galaxies.

AI.tldr: Multiphase outflow study in starburst galaxy ESO 484-036 reveals molecular gas dominates mass outflow rates, creating systematic discrepancy with cosmological simulations that underpredict cold gas production.
AI.motivation: To understand the multiphase nature of galactic outflows in starburst galaxies, particularly the relationship between ionized and molecular gas phases, and to compare observational results with cosmological simulations.
AI.method: Combined VLT/MUSE Hα observations for atomic ionized gas and ALMA CO(1-0) observations for cold molecular gas from the GECKOS survey, analyzing spatially resolved extraplanar emission to characterize outflow properties including velocities, mass outflow rates, and loading factors.
AI.result: Molecular gas dominates mass outflow rates (13-54 M⊙/yr) compared to ionized gas (1-5 M⊙/yr), with molecular gas detected up to 2.5 kpc from disc. Outflow velocities reach ~400 km/s with ballistic motion. Mass loading factors show η_mol ~1.5-6.2 vs η_ion ~0.1-0.6. Energy and momentum loading support starburst-driven outflow. Comparison reveals systematic 1 dex shift in mass-loading relations when including molecular gas, creating ~3.5 dex discrepancy with cosmological simulations.
AI.conclusion: Current cosmological simulations strongly underpredict cold gas production and short-range recycling flows in starburst galaxies. The outflow may regulate rather than permanently quench the gas reservoir despite short depletion times. Molecular gas plays a dominant role in mass outflow that is not adequately captured in existing models.

title: Numerical investigation of particle acceleration at interplanetary shocks: diffusive and superdiffusive scenarios
comment: The Article has been accepted in A&A
summary: Energetic particles are ubiquitous in space and astrophysical plasmas, and interplanetary shocks are widely regarded as one of the main particle accelerators in the heliosphere. Indeed, in-situ measurements typically show that energetic particle fluxes peak at the shock, indicating a local acceleration process. Furthermore, the time profile of energetic particle fluxes is highly influenced by particle transport properties upstream and downstream of the shock. By advancing previous numerical test-particle models that simulate the transport of monoenergetic particles around an infinite planar shock, in this work we add the acceleration of such particles via energy gains at each shock crossing, in a first-order Fermi-type mechanism. Moreover, the acceleration of a 70 keV particle population, namely the seed population, is reproduced by integrating a Langevin-type equation upstream and downstream of an infinite planar shock. Particles can diffuse in the simulation box via random "kicks", which belong either to a Gaussian distribution (normal diffusion) or to a Levy distribution (superdiffusion). We perform several simulations by varying the parameters of the model. The particle energy spectra in both diffusive and superdiffusive simulations are in remarkable agreement with the theoretical predictions.The output energetic particle densities have been compared with those observed by the ACE spacecraft during an interplanetary shock crossing on December 14, 2006. We show not only that particle fluxes in different energy bins reproduce very well the observed ones upstream and downstream when superdiffusion is at work, but also that anomalous, superdiffusive transport speeds up the acceleration process and leads to values of particle energies consistent with observations.

AI.tldr: Numerical model combines shock acceleration via first-order Fermi mechanism with anomalous superdiffusive transport, showing better agreement with spacecraft observations than normal diffusion models.
AI.motivation: To understand how energetic particles are accelerated at interplanetary shocks and how their transport properties (especially anomalous superdiffusion) affect acceleration efficiency and observed flux profiles.
AI.method: Advanced test-particle model with first-order Fermi acceleration at shock crossings, using Langevin equation for particle transport with both Gaussian (normal diffusion) and Levy (superdiffusion) distributions for random kicks.
AI.result: Superdiffusive transport speeds up acceleration process, produces particle energies consistent with observations, and reproduces ACE spacecraft flux measurements better than normal diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: Anomalous superdiffusive transport is crucial for explaining observed energetic particle acceleration at interplanetary shocks, providing better agreement with spacecraft data than traditional diffusive shock acceleration models.

title: Discovery of low-redshift analogues to "Little Red Dots" in DESI: A later evolutionary stage of compact LRDs?
comment: 9 pages, 3 main figures; accepted for publication in A&A Letters
summary: The James Webb Space Telescope (JWST) has recently discovered a population of compact, red sources at z > 4 known as "Little Red Dots" (LRDs). They are characterized by their V-shaped continuum spectra and prominent broad Balmer emission lines. As their underlying physical nature remains debated and direct study at high-redshift is challenging; therefore, we seek to identify and characterize LRD analogues in the low-redshift universe to constrain their properties and potential evolutionary pathways. We identified five candidates at z = 0.2-0.4 from the Dark Energy Spectroscopic Instrument (DESI) that exhibit spectral energy distributions (SEDs) and broad Balmer emission lines closely resembling their high-redshift counterparts. However, we find significant differences: our low-redshift sample occupies a different region on the Baldwin, Phillips \& Terlevich (BPT) diagram, and their stellar masses are significantly higher, suggesting a more substantial host galaxy contribution. These sources are not necessarily direct local analogues of high-redshift LRDs, but may represent later evolutionary stages of compact, rapidly accreting systems, or systems with related observational properties arising under different physical conditions. This sample provides a valuable laboratory for detailed follow-up studies to elucidate the nature of LRD-like phenomena.

AI.tldr: Low-redshift analogues of high-z "Little Red Dots" show similar spectral features but differ in BPT diagram position and stellar mass, suggesting they may represent later evolutionary stages rather than direct counterparts.
AI.motivation: To understand the nature of high-redshift "Little Red Dots" (LRDs) discovered by JWST by identifying and characterizing low-redshift analogues, since direct study at high redshift is challenging and their physical nature remains debated.
AI.method: Identified five candidate LRD analogues at z = 0.2-0.4 from DESI data based on spectral energy distributions (SEDs) and broad Balmer emission lines resembling high-redshift LRDs, then analyzed their properties including BPT diagram positions and stellar masses.
AI.result: Low-redshift candidates show similar V-shaped continuum spectra and broad Balmer lines, but occupy different regions on the BPT diagram and have significantly higher stellar masses than high-z LRDs, indicating more substantial host galaxy contributions.
AI.conclusion: Low-redshift sources are not direct analogues of high-z LRDs but may represent later evolutionary stages of compact, rapidly accreting systems or systems with similar observational properties under different physical conditions, providing a valuable laboratory for follow-up studies.

title: An FUor-like Outbursting Class I Protostar in NGC 7538
comment: accepted for publication in RAA
summary: We report on the discovery of an FUor-like Class I protostar in NGC~7538. The source, named NGC~7538~MIR, exhibited a giant luminosity burst ($ΔK_s\sim5$) and a prolonged high-luminosity state lasting at least five years. Its mid-infrared (mid-IR) light curves, constructed from WISE/NEOWISE multiepoch data, presented a rapid rise and slight fading after the peak, placing this event among long-duration eruptive phenomena observed in protostars, for example, FUor-type events. The evolution of W1/W2 luminosity and $W1-W2$ color can be naturally split into three phases, pre-burst, burst and post-burst, suggesting that different physical processes may dominate in the three phases. The evolution of NGC~7538~MIR is consistent with a transition from variability influenced by circumstellar extinction (pre-burst) to a phase with greatly enhanced accretion luminosity (burst), and followed by a gradual relaxation of the circumstellar environment (post-burst). Overall, the observed IR variability of NGC~7538~MIR is consistent with an FUor-like accretion event occurred at an early evolutionary stage, highlighting the importance of long-term IR monitoring for identifying episodic accretion events in deeply embedded protostars.

AI.tldr: Discovery of NGC~7538~MIR, an FUor-like Class I protostar exhibiting a giant luminosity burst (ΔK_s∼5) with prolonged high-luminosity state lasting ≥5 years, identified through WISE/NEOWISE mid-IR monitoring.
AI.motivation: To identify and characterize episodic accretion events in deeply embedded protostars through long-term infrared monitoring, particularly focusing on FUor-like outbursts that reveal important physical processes during early stellar evolution.
AI.method: Analysis of mid-infrared light curves constructed from WISE/NEOWISE multiepoch data, tracking luminosity evolution (W1/W2 bands) and color (W1-W2) changes over time to identify burst phases and physical transitions.
AI.result: Discovery of three distinct evolutionary phases: pre-burst (circumstellar extinction-dominated variability), burst (greatly enhanced accretion luminosity with ΔK_s∼5), and post-burst (gradual circumstellar environment relaxation). The event lasted at least five years with rapid rise and slight fading after peak.
AI.conclusion: NGC~7538~MIR represents an FUor-like accretion event at an early evolutionary stage, demonstrating the value of long-term IR monitoring for identifying episodic accretion in embedded protostars and revealing transitions between different physical regimes during outbursts.

title: Impact of Baseline, Cadence, and Host Contamination on AGN Variability Metrics: A Systematic Study with ZTF
summary: Variability in active galactic nuclei (AGN) probes the physics of accretion onto supermassive black holes. This variability is characterized using metrics derived from the flux distributions of temporally separated epochs. We studied the stability of two variability metrics, the Stetson index "J" and the smoothness "s", against baseline, cadence, and host galaxy contamination. We studied 23 nearby AGNs using Zwicky Transient Facility's Data Release 24. Both metrics are robust to baseline variations of $\sim 2$ years. However, s is sensitive to cadence, showing variations $\gtrsim 40\%$, while J shows minor variations $\lesssim10\%$. We studied the host galaxy impact using Mrk 493 as a representative case. We found that J remains unchanged after host subtraction, while s increases. We concluded that J is a robust tool for characterizing AGN variability, while s should be interpreted with caution.

AI.tldr: The Stetson index J is a robust metric for AGN variability characterization, showing minimal sensitivity to baseline, cadence, and host galaxy contamination, while the smoothness metric s is significantly affected by cadence and host subtraction.
AI.motivation: To evaluate the stability and reliability of two variability metrics (Stetson index J and smoothness s) for AGN characterization against observational factors including baseline duration, cadence, and host galaxy contamination.
AI.method: Analyzed 23 nearby AGNs using Zwicky Transient Facility's Data Release 24. Studied metric stability against baseline variations (~2 years), cadence effects, and host galaxy contamination using Mrk 493 as a representative case with host subtraction analysis.
AI.result: Both metrics are robust to baseline variations of ~2 years. Smoothness s shows significant sensitivity to cadence (variations ≥40%), while Stetson index J shows minor variations (≤10%). Host subtraction analysis reveals J remains unchanged but s increases after host galaxy removal.
AI.conclusion: The Stetson index J is a robust tool for characterizing AGN variability, while the smoothness metric s should be interpreted with caution due to its sensitivity to cadence and host galaxy contamination.

title: Photometric Metallicities for 367,324 stars of Omega Centauri
comment: 13 pages, 12 figures, accepted for publication in ApJS
summary: Omega Centauri is the most massive and chemically complex multi-population globular cluster with a wide metallicity range that has been extensively studied photometrically and spectroscopically. Using the wide metallicity range of omega Cen, HST photometry (F275W, F336W, F435W, F625W), and MUSE spectroscopy ([M/H]), we derive [M/H]- and M_{F625W}-dependent stellar loci to estimate photometric metallicities from HST colors. Our tests yield metallicity precisions of 0.10\,dex for giants and 0.22\,dex for fainter dwarfs. We construct a photometric metallicity catalog from simultaneous F336W, F435W, and F625W observations (plus F275W where available), containing 20,778 giants and 346,546 dwarfs. A subsample of 20,533 giants is used to study the spatial metallicity distribution and gradient. We find no significant metallicity gradient within the half-light radius, consistent with previous work. Moreover, the previously reported ring-like structure is less pronounced in our data, and no physically significant, irregular two-dimensional metallicity pattern is detected, indicating that the stellar subpopulations are well mixed within the half-light radius. Our catalog significantly extends the metallicity sample of omega Cen, and this approach can be applied to other HST data to estimate photometric metallicities.

AI.tldr: Researchers developed a method to estimate photometric metallicities for Omega Centauri stars using HST photometry and MUSE spectroscopy, creating a catalog of over 367,000 stars and finding well-mixed stellar populations within the cluster's half-light radius.
AI.motivation: Omega Centauri is the most massive and chemically complex globular cluster with a wide metallicity range, but obtaining spectroscopic metallicities for large samples is observationally expensive. The motivation is to develop a photometric method to estimate metallicities for a much larger sample of stars using available HST photometry.
AI.method: Using HST photometry (F275W, F336W, F435W, F625W filters) and MUSE spectroscopy ([M/H] measurements), the researchers derived metallicity- and magnitude-dependent stellar loci. They created empirical relationships between HST colors and metallicities, then applied these to estimate photometric metallicities for stars with simultaneous observations in F336W, F435W, and F625W (plus F275W where available).
AI.result: The method achieved metallicity precisions of 0.10 dex for giants and 0.22 dex for fainter dwarfs. The resulting catalog contains 20,778 giants and 346,546 dwarfs (total 367,324 stars). Analysis of 20,533 giants showed no significant metallicity gradient within the half-light radius, and the previously reported ring-like structure was less pronounced, indicating well-mixed stellar subpopulations.
AI.conclusion: The photometric metallicity catalog significantly extends the metallicity sample for Omega Centauri, providing a valuable resource for studying its complex stellar populations. The method demonstrates that stellar subpopulations are well mixed within the half-light radius, and the approach can be applied to other HST data to estimate photometric metallicities for large stellar samples.

title: Disk-like galaxies at 4 < z < 7.7 : JWST/NIRCam morphologies revealed by denoising VAE-GCNN classification
comment: 5 pages, 4 figs; MNRAS,Lett., in press
summary: Understanding the prevalence of disk-like galaxies at very high redshifts is crucial for constraining the early formation of angular momentum-supported structures. The advent of JWST now permits rest-frame UV and optical morphological studies deep into cosmic epochs where disks have traditionally been considered uncommon. We apply an identical denoising VAE-GCNN classification pipeline to multi-filter JWST/NIRCam cutouts in order to obtain homogeneous, morphology-based disk fractions across the sample. Our approach comprises two steps: (i) a U-Net Variational Autoencoder (VAE) is trained to remove astrophysical and instrumental contaminants while preserving intrinsic morphology, and (ii) a rotation - and reflection - equivariant GCNN classifier is applied to the denoised cutouts to distinguish disk-like galaxies from non-disks. We determine the fraction of disk-like galaxies as 0.34 for a sample of JWST 100 galaxies over the redshift range 4 < z < 7.7, also in dependence on the galaxy mass range. Our GCNN-based morphological analysis indicates that disk-like systems constitute a significant fraction of the considered high-redshift population and underscore the importance of such studies for the models of disk formation in the first billion years.

AI.tldr: JWST study uses denoising VAE and GCNN classifier to find 34% of galaxies at z=4-7.7 are disk-like, challenging traditional views of early galaxy formation.
AI.motivation: To understand the prevalence of disk-like galaxies at very high redshifts (z=4-7.7) and constrain early formation of angular momentum-supported structures, which is crucial for models of disk formation in the first billion years.
AI.method: Two-step pipeline: (1) U-Net Variational Autoencoder (VAE) trained to remove astrophysical and instrumental contaminants while preserving intrinsic morphology; (2) rotation- and reflection-equivariant Graph Convolutional Neural Network (GCNN) classifier applied to denoised JWST/NIRCam cutouts to distinguish disk-like galaxies from non-disks.
AI.result: Disk fraction of 0.34 (34%) for JWST sample of 100 galaxies over redshift range 4 < z < 7.7, with dependence on galaxy mass range. Disk-like systems constitute significant fraction of high-redshift population.
AI.conclusion: Disk-like galaxies are more prevalent at very high redshifts than traditionally considered, underscoring the importance of morphological studies for understanding early disk formation and challenging existing models of galaxy evolution in the first billion years.

title: Radiation doses during extreme solar energetic particle events
comment: In press in Phil. Trans. R. Soc A
summary: Ions and electrons accelerated to high energies during flares and coronal mass ejections at the Sun may escape the solar atmosphere and, guided by the interplanetary magnetic fields, propagate through space to near-Earth locations. These Solar Energetic Particles (SEPs) can be detected directly by spacecraft instrumentation. The highest energy SEPs may also propagate through the geomagnetic field and precipitate to low atmospheric heights, producing secondary particles including neutrons and protons that trigger the formation of cosmogenic radionuclides. The space weather effects associated with the SEP ion population (for the most part protons) consist principally of radiation risk to aviation, humans in space and spacecraft. This paper focusses on the risks to aviation and astronauts and emphasizes how the parameters of the SEP event, including fluence and spectrum, affect radiation doses. Space weather effects for events that took place in recent decades, for which a large body of measurements and models exist, are discussed. SEP events of extreme magnitudes, such as those extrapolated from radionuclide data from the distant past are then considered and first estimates of the associated radiation at aviation altitudes and in space presented. These are worst-case estimates derived within the assumption that the radionuclide spike was caused by a single SEP event and that the particle spectrum and geomagnetic conditions were the same during all events.

AI.tldr: This paper analyzes radiation risks from Solar Energetic Particles (SEPs) to aviation and astronauts, examining both recent events and extreme historical events extrapolated from radionuclide data.
AI.motivation: To understand and quantify radiation risks posed by Solar Energetic Particles to aviation and astronauts, particularly focusing on how event parameters (fluence, spectrum) affect radiation doses, and to estimate risks from extreme historical SEP events.
AI.method: Analysis of SEP events from recent decades using existing measurements and models, combined with extrapolation from radionuclide data from distant past events to estimate radiation doses at aviation altitudes and in space.
AI.result: The paper presents radiation dose estimates for both recent SEP events and extreme historical events, showing how event parameters significantly affect radiation risks, with worst-case estimates derived under specific assumptions about particle spectra and geomagnetic conditions.
AI.conclusion: SEP events pose significant radiation risks to aviation and astronauts, with extreme historical events potentially causing substantially higher doses than recent events, highlighting the importance of understanding SEP event parameters for space weather risk assessment.

title: A simple yet effective model of galaxy mergers
comment: 22 pages, 16 figures. arXiv admin note: text overlap with arXiv:2501.10047
summary: In the context of the hierarchical formation of galaxies, we investigated the role played by mergers in shaping the scale relations of galaxies, that is the projections of their Fundamental Plane onto the \IeRe, \IeSig, \MRa\ and \Lsig\ planes. To this aim, we developed a simple model of multiple dry mergers among galaxies by suitably combing the formalism and properties of the so-called infall models of galaxy formation and evolution with the formalism of the scalar Virial Theorem. In this context, we mimicked the hierarchical formation of galaxies and generated simple models of galaxies undergoing a number mergers in the course of their evolution. The results are used to interpret the large scale simulations and the companion scale relations from observational and theoretical perspectives. The aim is to interpret the observational data of the MANGA and WINGS samples and the results of theoretical detailed numerical cosmo-hydro-dynamical simulations, such as Illustris-TNG100. In this context, we derived the above scale relations for our theoretical models and compared them with the observational counterparts from the MANGA and WINGS database, (and indirectly the large scale simulations of Illustris-TNG100). The multiple dry merging mechanism is able to explain all the main characteristics of the observed scale relations of galaxies, such as slopes, scatters, curvatures and zones of exclusion. The distribution of galaxies in these planes is continuously changing across time because of the merging activity and other physical processes, such as star formation, quenching, energy feedback, and so forth.} The precision of the present simple merger theory is comparable with that obtained by the modern cosmo-hydro-dynamical simulations, with the advantage of providing a rapid exploratory response on the consequences engendered by different physical effects.

AI.tldr: Multiple dry mergers explain galaxy scale relations (Fundamental Plane projections) with precision comparable to modern cosmological simulations.
AI.motivation: To understand how mergers shape galaxy scale relations (Fundamental Plane projections) in hierarchical galaxy formation and interpret observational data from MANGA/WINGS samples and simulations like Illustris-TNG100.
AI.method: Developed a simple model of multiple dry mergers combining infall galaxy formation models with scalar Virial Theorem formalism to mimic hierarchical galaxy formation through mergers.
AI.result: Multiple dry merging explains all main characteristics of observed scale relations: slopes, scatters, curvatures, and zones of exclusion. Model precision comparable to modern cosmo-hydro-dynamical simulations.
AI.conclusion: Dry mergers play a key role in shaping galaxy scale relations, with simple merger theory providing rapid exploratory analysis comparable to complex simulations.

title: Stellar nucleosynthesis in the era of large surveys: s-process polluted binaries in GALAH DR4
comment: Under review in A&A
summary: Binary interactions during the AGB phase can lead to the formation of chemically peculiar stars with overabundances of s-process elements. Only a few hundreds of these stars have been subject to detailed chemical or dynamical studies. This work aims at compiling a systematic sample of s-process-polluted candidates using GALAH DR4. We also want to compare their properties with those of confirmed s-polluted stars to have stronger evidence of their nature. GALAH DR4 uses neural networks and automatic spectral analysis methods as well as data of a lower spectral resolution than normally used to characterise these objects. Because of this, we built a validation sample, for which we obtained UVES@VLT and HERMES@Mercator high-resolution spectra. We compare our stellar parameters and abundances with those of the survey and use this validation to define the thresholds that a star in GALAH DR4 must pass to be flagged as a good s-process-rich candidate. Based on our comparisons, we define thresholds on [s/Fe], [Y/Fe], [Zr/Fe], [Ba/Fe], and [La/Fe]. We identified 1073 stars in GALAH DR4 that are good candidates to be s-process polluted stars, covering a broad parameter space. They share many similarities with the samples of confirmed s-rich stars, especially their ratios of heavy over light s-elements ([hs/ls]), which strengthen our confidence in the purity of the sample. We find that only 7% of the candidates have measured orbital periods and eccentricities, limiting for now a full comparison with confirmed Ba and related stars. However, their binary fraction is, as expected, higher than the one we found for the full GALAH DR4 catalogue. Our sample of candidates is almost five times larger than the number of currently confirmed polluted stars. This and the fact that it has been homogeneously treated by GALAH open very interesting avenues to confront nucleosynthesis and binary evolution models.

AI.tldr: Researchers compiled a systematic sample of s-process-polluted star candidates from GALAH DR4 using neural network analysis, validated with high-resolution spectra, and identified 1073 promising candidates with chemical signatures similar to confirmed s-rich stars.
AI.motivation: Binary interactions during AGB phase produce chemically peculiar stars with s-process element overabundances, but only a few hundred have been studied in detail. The study aims to systematically identify s-process-polluted candidates using GALAH DR4 data and compare them with confirmed s-rich stars to strengthen evidence of their nature.
AI.method: Used GALAH DR4 data analyzed with neural networks and automatic spectral analysis methods. Built a validation sample with high-resolution UVES@VLT and HERMES@Mercator spectra to compare stellar parameters and abundances. Defined thresholds on [s/Fe], [Y/Fe], [Zr/Fe], [Ba/Fe], and [La/Fe] to identify s-process-rich candidates.
AI.result: Identified 1073 stars in GALAH DR4 as good s-process polluted candidates covering broad parameter space. Candidates share similarities with confirmed s-rich stars, particularly in heavy-to-light s-element ratios ([hs/ls]), indicating sample purity. Binary fraction is higher than full GALAH DR4 catalogue, though only 7% have measured orbital periods and eccentricities.
AI.conclusion: The sample of 1073 candidates is nearly five times larger than currently confirmed polluted stars and homogeneously treated by GALAH, providing valuable data to test nucleosynthesis and binary evolution models. The validation approach and chemical similarity to confirmed s-rich stars strengthen confidence in candidate identification.

title: FAST and Dark: A catalogue of Dark Galaxy Candidates within 50 Mpc
comment: 21 pages, 14 figures, 5 tables. Accepted for publication in MNRAS
summary: Using the first data release of the Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical radio Telescope (FAST) All-Sky HI survey (FASHI), we compile a catalogue of 70 dark galaxy candidates (DGCs) within 50 Mpc. We select DGCs without an identified optical counterpart at a limiting g-band magnitude of ~ 28 mag arcsec^-2 in the DESI Legacy Survey, using both automatic cross-checking with optical catalogues and visual inspection of the colour images. After validating our DGCs, excluding potential spurious detections, issues in the registered position of the HI sources, and possible Radio Frequency Interferences (RFIs), we analyse their distribution over the surveyed sky, HI mass, linewidths, and inferred distance. They appear evenly distributed across the surveyed area, with no apparent bias to isolation. We did not find any DGC within the Local Volume (11 Mpc) in the sky surveyed by this first release of FASHI. We compare the observed properties of DGCs with those of galaxies with optical counterparts, finding that DGCs tend to have higher linewidths for a given HI mass. We discuss our DGCs in light of theoretical works, and compare them with other observational samples from previous HI surveys. This work presents a catalogue of dark galaxy candidates, which can serve as a basis for follow-up studies.

AI.tldr: FAST telescope survey identifies 70 dark galaxy candidates without optical counterparts, showing higher linewidths for given HI mass compared to normal galaxies.
AI.motivation: To systematically search for and characterize dark galaxy candidates using the first data release of the FAST All-Sky HI survey, addressing the existence and properties of galaxies dominated by dark matter with minimal stellar content.
AI.method: Used FAST All-Sky HI survey data to identify HI sources without optical counterparts in DESI Legacy Survey (limiting magnitude ~28 mag arcsec^-2), employing both automated cross-checking with optical catalogs and visual inspection of color images, followed by validation to exclude spurious detections, position errors, and RFI contamination.
AI.result: Compiled catalog of 70 dark galaxy candidates within 50 Mpc, evenly distributed across surveyed area with no isolation bias; no candidates found within Local Volume (11 Mpc); DGCs show systematically higher linewidths for given HI mass compared to galaxies with optical counterparts.
AI.conclusion: The study presents a robust catalog of dark galaxy candidates suitable for follow-up observations, revealing distinct kinematic properties that suggest different formation or evolutionary pathways compared to optically visible galaxies, contributing to understanding galaxy formation in dark matter-dominated regimes.

title: Enhanced activity in close dual-AGN systems in the local Universe
comment: 16 pages, 10 figures. Accepted for publication in A&A
summary: We present the study of an X-ray selected sample of active galactic nuclei (AGN) in pairs at projected spatial separations 1 <$ r_p$/kpc < 100 at z < 0.1, using XMM-Newton and Chandra data. The pair sample is derived from an initial pool of approximately 2,000 X-ray-selected AGN, and is composed of both AGN-AGN pairs (so called dual AGN) and AGN-galaxy pairs. From this selection, we find that approximately 10% of AGN reside in pairs, and about 4% are paired with another AGN. We performed a detailed X-ray and SDSS optical spectral analysis for AGN in duals and X-ray analysis for AGN in AGN-galaxy pairs, to characterise their absorption properties and investigate the possible triggering mechanisms. We then investigated how obscuration, luminosity, and Eddington ratio depend on projected separation $r_p$. Amongst all AGN in pairs, we found that ~55% are obscured (with hydrogen column density $N_H$ > $10^{22}$ cm$^{-2}$), amongst which ~6% are Compton-thick ($N_H$ > $10^{24}$ cm$^{-2}$). The fraction of absorbed AGN is significantly higher in late-stage mergers ($r_p$ < 30 kpc) compared to early-stage mergers ($r_p$ > 60 kpc). Amongst the AGN in pairs, we also observed an average excess of AGN pairs with respect to a control sample of inactive galaxies in pairs, and that such excess significantly increases with decreasing $r_p$ only for obscured AGN. Finally, in dual-AGN systems, both the bolometric luminosity and the Eddington ratio of the less massive black hole in the pair increase as the separation decreases. These findings suggest that mergers may have an important role in triggering AGN accretion and activity.

AI.tldr: Study of X-ray selected AGN pairs at z<0.1 finds ~10% of AGN in pairs, with ~4% being dual AGN; obscured fraction increases at smaller separations, suggesting mergers trigger AGN activity.
AI.motivation: To investigate the role of galaxy mergers in triggering AGN activity by studying X-ray selected AGN in pairs at different projected separations, and to characterize their absorption properties and accretion behavior.
AI.method: Used XMM-Newton and Chandra data to select AGN pairs from ~2000 X-ray-selected AGN at z<0.1 with projected separations 1-100 kpc. Performed detailed X-ray and SDSS optical spectral analysis for dual AGN and X-ray analysis for AGN-galaxy pairs. Analyzed obscuration, luminosity, and Eddington ratio dependencies on projected separation, and compared with control sample of inactive galaxy pairs.
AI.result: ~10% of AGN reside in pairs, ~4% are dual AGN. ~55% of AGN in pairs are obscured (N_H > 10²² cm⁻²), with ~6% Compton-thick. Obscured fraction significantly higher in late-stage mergers (r_p < 30 kpc) vs early-stage (r_p > 60 kpc). Excess of AGN pairs relative to inactive galaxy pairs increases with decreasing separation for obscured AGN. In dual AGN, bolometric luminosity and Eddington ratio of less massive black hole increase as separation decreases.
AI.conclusion: Mergers play an important role in triggering AGN accretion and activity, with stronger effects at smaller separations where gas inflows and obscuration increase, particularly enhancing accretion onto the less massive black hole in dual systems.

title: Do galaxy mergers increase star formation and turbulence at cosmic noon?
comment: 15 pages, 12 figures, 2 tables, submitted to MNRAS
summary: Mergers and interactions can significantly affect the morphological and dynamical properties of galaxies, however the impact of mergers on turbulence at $z > 1$ has not been observationally constrained. In this work we use the interaction strength parameter $Q_P$ to identify likely interacting and isolated galaxies at cosmic noon ($z \sim 1-2$) within the KMOS\textsuperscript{3D} integral field spectroscopy survey, utilising redshifts from the 3D-HST, CANDELS and UVCANDELS surveys. For $186$ galaxies, we measure deconvolved H$α$ kinematics, including velocity dispersion, using a spatially non-parametric approach to account for observational effects in the dynamically diverse range of galaxies. We compare offsets in H$α$ flux, star formation rate (SFR), dust attenuations, and velocity dispersion of likely interacting galaxies to isolated control galaxies matched in mass and lookback time. We find increased H$α$ fluxes and SFRs in the likely interacting sample at the level of $\sim 0.1$ dex, a similar enhancement to studies of local pairs. In contrast, we find no significant increase in the level of velocity dispersion in interacting galaxies compared to their controls. The lack of increase in dispersion may reflect a combination of physical and observational factors, including limits to increasing turbulent motions in an already turbulent medium and spectral resolution limits.

AI.tldr: Study finds interacting galaxies at z~1-2 show enhanced star formation but no significant increase in velocity dispersion compared to isolated galaxies.
AI.motivation: To observationally constrain the impact of galaxy mergers and interactions on turbulence at cosmic noon (z~1-2), where such effects have not been previously measured.
AI.method: Used interaction strength parameter Q_P to identify interacting and isolated galaxies from KMOS³D survey, measured Hα kinematics via non-parametric deconvolution, compared interacting galaxies to mass- and lookback-time-matched controls.
AI.result: Interacting galaxies show ~0.1 dex increases in Hα flux and SFR (similar to local pairs), but no significant increase in velocity dispersion compared to isolated controls.
AI.conclusion: The lack of increased velocity dispersion in interacting galaxies may reflect physical limits to turbulence enhancement in already turbulent media and/or spectral resolution limitations.

title: Structure and Large-Scale Kinematics of Young Stellar Populations in the NGC 6357 and NGC 6334 Giant Molecular Cloud Complex
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures, submitted to AAS Journals. Full X-ray catalog table available by request
summary: We map the three-dimensional structure and large-scale kinematics of the young stellar populations in the G352 giant molecular cloud (GMC) complex. In radio and infrared images, G352 appears as long filament extending ~$3^{\circ}$ (~150 pc) parallel to the Galactic midplane. It connects the NGC 6357 and NGC 6334 giant H II regions and the GM1-24 compact H II region. We identify 1727 stellar members of G352 via matching large catalogs of Chandra X-ray point sources and Spitzer mid-infrared excess sources to the Gaia DR3 astrometric catalog. Our catalog of 11,470 X-ray point sources ranks among the three largest contiguous X-ray survey datasets ever assembled for a massive star-forming complex. We revise the mean heliocentric distance of G352 to $1670\pm 80$ pc, with the median parallaxes of seven constituent groups exhibiting a trend toward increasing distance with decreasing Galactic longitude. We identify two foreground stellar groups superimposed on NGC 6357 that may belong to the Sag OB4 association. The three massive clusters in NGC 6357 exhibit peculiar velocities that trail Galactic circular motion by ${\sim}8$ km/s, while the stars associated with NGC 6334 are more consistent with a circular orbit. GM1-24 has a distinct proper motion and smaller parallax compared to NGC 6334. The steep pitch angle of the GMC filament into the sky appears inconsistent with a spiral arm. The various stellar groups are not gravitationally bound to each other, making G352 a proto-OB association.

AI.tldr: 3D mapping of young stellar populations in the G352 giant molecular cloud complex reveals a 150 pc filament connecting three H II regions, with revised distance of 1670±80 pc and kinematic evidence for a proto-OB association rather than a spiral arm structure.
AI.motivation: To understand the three-dimensional structure and large-scale kinematics of young stellar populations in the G352 giant molecular cloud complex, which appears as a long filament connecting major H II regions and may provide insights into star formation processes and Galactic structure.
AI.method: Identified 1727 stellar members by cross-matching Chandra X-ray point sources and Spitzer mid-infrared excess sources with Gaia DR3 astrometric catalog. Used parallax measurements from Gaia to determine distances and proper motions to analyze kinematics. Assembled one of the largest contiguous X-ray survey datasets for a massive star-forming complex (11,470 X-ray sources).
AI.result: Revised mean heliocentric distance to 1670±80 pc with distance gradient along Galactic longitude. Identified two foreground groups possibly belonging to Sag OB4. Found NGC 6357 clusters trail Galactic circular motion by ~8 km/s while NGC 6334 stars follow circular orbits. GM1-24 has distinct proper motion and smaller parallax. The filament's steep pitch angle is inconsistent with spiral arm structure, and stellar groups are not gravitationally bound.
AI.conclusion: G352 represents a proto-OB association rather than a spiral arm structure, with multiple stellar groups exhibiting independent kinematics and not being gravitationally bound to each other, suggesting a complex star formation history across the molecular cloud complex.

title: Understanding the regulation of star formation within TNG100 galaxies on kpc-scales using machine learning I: Global versus local
comment: Preprint submitted to Astronomy & Computing
summary: We apply Random Forest and XGBoost machine learning algorithms to determine which galaxy properties most effectively predict star formation and quenching in simulated galaxies. Using spatially-resolved data from approximately 63,000 annular bins across 6,189 TNG100 galaxies, we train classification models to predict quenching states and regression models to predict star formation rate surface densities. Despite their different algorithmic approaches, both methods produce consistent feature importance rankings, with XGBoost distributing importance more evenly among correlated features. For central galaxies and high-mass satellites, black hole mass dominates quenching predictions, consistent with quenching via active galactic nuclei (AGN) feedback. Classification of low-mass satellites shows overwhelming importance for halo mass, indicating environmental quenching. Star formation predictions are dominated by local stellar mass surface density across all star-forming galaxy types, confirming that active star formation is a local process while quenching is driven by global properties.

AI.tldr: Random Forest and XGBoost applied to TNG100 simulated galaxies reveal black hole mass dominates quenching predictions for centrals/high-mass satellites (AGN feedback), halo mass dominates for low-mass satellites (environmental quenching), and stellar mass surface density dominates star formation predictions across all star-forming galaxies.
AI.motivation: To determine which galaxy properties most effectively predict star formation and quenching in simulated galaxies using machine learning approaches, and to understand the physical drivers behind these processes.
AI.method: Applied Random Forest and XGBoost algorithms to spatially-resolved data from approximately 63,000 annular bins across 6,189 TNG100 galaxies. Used classification models to predict quenching states and regression models to predict star formation rate surface densities. Analyzed feature importance rankings from both methods.
AI.result: Both Random Forest and XGBoost produced consistent feature importance rankings, with XGBoost distributing importance more evenly among correlated features. For central galaxies and high-mass satellites, black hole mass dominated quenching predictions (consistent with AGN feedback). For low-mass satellites, halo mass dominated (indicating environmental quenching). Star formation predictions were dominated by local stellar mass surface density across all star-forming galaxy types.
AI.conclusion: Active star formation is primarily a local process driven by stellar mass surface density, while quenching is driven by global properties: AGN feedback (black hole mass) for centrals/high-mass satellites and environmental effects (halo mass) for low-mass satellites. The consistency between Random Forest and XGBoost validates the robustness of these findings.

title: Tracing evolutionary pathways of bar-driven quenching in local Universe disc galaxies
comment: Accepted for publication in Astronomy & Astrophysics, 14 pages, 8 figures
summary: Bars play an integral role in regulating star formation (SF) in spiral galaxies, from triggering central starbursts to driving quenching. The diverse SF morphologies observed in local barred galaxies reflect different evolutionary stages of the bar, motivating studies across these stages. Here we study 12 nearby barred galaxies (z=0.01-0.06) identified as centrally quenched galaxies (having extended star-forming discs but quenched inner regions) by leveraging the differences in SFRs between the MPA-JHU and GSWLC catalogues. However, they exhibit residual central emission in the SDSS 3" fibre spectral region. Emission line analysis shows that this emission originates from either ongoing SF or LINER-like activity, suggesting diverse central ionization mechanisms. Using spatially resolved UV-optical colour maps from SDSS (r-band) and GALEX (FUV and NUV band) imaging data, we find that discs are star-forming and bluer in colour (NUV-r < 4 mag) while the bulge and bar regions are systematically redder (NUV-r > 4 mag) and dominated by older stellar populations. The NUV-r radial colour profiles show a clear transition from red to blue colours at the bar end with a corresponding median stellar age of ~ 1 Gyr. Compared to fully centrally quenched barred galaxies from our earlier work which lack SDSS fibre emission, these galaxies remain systematically bluer at similar radii, despite showing NUV-r > 4 mag inside the bar suggesting an intermediate stage in bar-driven quenching. We also estimate black hole masses associated with kinetic-mode AGN feedback and find them below the threshold (logM_BH < 8.0). Adding this with the presence of pseudo bulges, our results support bars as the primary drivers of quenching, with these galaxies representing an evolutionary phase just before their inner regions are completely quenched.

AI.tldr: Study of 12 nearby barred galaxies showing intermediate stage of bar-driven quenching, with star-forming discs but redder, older bulge/bar regions, suggesting bars as primary quenching drivers before complete central quenching.
AI.motivation: To understand the evolutionary stages of bar-driven quenching in spiral galaxies, specifically examining galaxies that appear centrally quenched but show residual central emission, representing an intermediate phase between active star formation and complete quenching.
AI.method: Analyzed 12 nearby barred galaxies (z=0.01-0.06) using SDSS and GALEX imaging data to create spatially resolved UV-optical color maps (NUV-r). Performed emission line analysis of SDSS 3" fiber spectra to identify ionization mechanisms. Compared to fully quenched barred galaxies from previous work and estimated black hole masses for AGN feedback assessment.
AI.result: Discs are star-forming and blue (NUV-r < 4 mag) while bulge/bar regions are systematically redder (NUV-r > 4 mag) with older stellar populations. NUV-r radial profiles show transition from red to blue at bar ends with median stellar age ~1 Gyr. These galaxies are systematically bluer than fully quenched barred galaxies at similar radii. Black hole masses (logM_BH < 8.0) are below kinetic-mode AGN feedback threshold.
AI.conclusion: Bars are the primary drivers of quenching in these galaxies, which represent an intermediate evolutionary phase just before complete central quenching. The presence of pseudo bulges and sub-threshold black hole masses further supports bar-driven rather than AGN-driven quenching mechanisms.

title: Age bimodality in pseudo-bulges of barred spiral galaxies: Bar-driven evolution across cosmic time
comment: 12 pages, 7 figures, Accepted in MNRAS
summary: We investigate the stellar population properties of pseudo-bulges in barred galaxies drawn from the Sloan Digital Sky Survey (SDSS DR7) to assess how bars regulate central star formation and secular evolution. Our sample comprises barred spiral and barred lenticular (S0) galaxies with reliable spectroscopic indices obtained from multicomponent structural decompositions. Stellar ages and recent star formation are traced using the 4000 Å break strength ($D_{n}(4000)$) and the Balmer absorption index ($Hδ_{A}$), complemented by bulge, bar, and disc colours. Barred spirals show a clear bimodality in $D_{n}(4000)$, with peaks at $D_{n}(4000)\sim1.3$ and $\sim1.8$. Low-$D_{n}(4000)$ pseudo-bulges exhibit strong $Hδ_{A}$ absorption, blue colours, and high specific star-formation rates, indicating young, actively growing centres. High-$D_{n}(4000)$ systems instead show weak $Hδ_{A}$, red colours, and low sSFR, consistent with older, quenched pseudo-bulges. Barred S0s display an old-bulge-dominated distribution, suggesting that gas-poor barred spirals transition into S0s following disc-wide quenching. We also find elevated AGN incidence among old pseudo-bulges. These trends support a scenario in which bars funnel gas inward to build pseudo-bulges and later suppress central star formation by depleting or stabilising the inflow. IFU observations show that bars assemble cold nuclear discs that age and quench over time, while high-redshift imaging confirms that bars are already present at $z\sim4$, implying that this evolutionary cycle operates across cosmic time. The strong correspondence between stellar age, colour, and structure indicates that bar-driven secular evolution governs both the growth and quenching of central components, linking blue barred spirals to red S0 galaxies.

AI.tldr: Bars in galaxies regulate central star formation and secular evolution, with pseudo-bulges showing bimodal age distributions that trace growth and quenching phases, linking barred spirals to S0 galaxies.
AI.motivation: To understand how bars regulate central star formation and secular evolution in galaxies by examining stellar population properties of pseudo-bulges in barred galaxies.
AI.method: Analysis of barred spiral and S0 galaxies from SDSS DR7 using spectroscopic indices (D_n(4000) and Hδ_A) from multicomponent structural decompositions, complemented by bulge, bar, and disc colours.
AI.result: Barred spirals show bimodal D_n(4000) distribution (peaks at ~1.3 and ~1.8) indicating young/active vs. old/quenched pseudo-bulges. Barred S0s are old-bulge-dominated. Elevated AGN incidence in old pseudo-bulges. Bars funnel gas to build pseudo-bulges then suppress star formation.
AI.conclusion: Bar-driven secular evolution governs both growth and quenching of central components, linking blue barred spirals to red S0 galaxies, with this cycle operating across cosmic time as evidenced by high-redshift bar observations.
